content.text: A pull request (PR) — called a merge request (MR) on GitLab — is a formal mechanism for proposing, reviewing, and integrating code changes from a feature branch into a target branch. Pull requests are the collaboration surface of modern software teams: they surface the proposed changes, host the review conversation, trigger automated checks, and record the decision to merge with a persistent audit trail.
What It Is

A pull request is a platform-level feature (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, Azure DevOps) built on top of Git. It associates a source branch, a target branch, a description, a set of reviewers, and a thread of review comments with a proposed merge operation. When the PR is approved and all required status checks pass, the platform performs the merge according to the chosen strategy (merge commit, squash, or rebase).

Unlike raw git merge, a pull request creates a persistent record — even after the branch is deleted and the merge is completed, the PR remains browsable, showing the original diff, every comment, who approved, and the CI results. This makes pull requests the primary accountability mechanism for code quality in collaborative teams.

In AI development workflows, pull requests serve additional roles: they are the integration point for prompt changes, model configuration updates, evaluation results, and infrastructure-as-code modifications. A PR that updates an LLM system prompt or changes a retrieval pipeline should trigger the same rigorous review as any code change.
Why It Matters

Shipping code without review is an asymmetric risk: the cost of a missed bug, security vulnerability, or architectural mistake can far exceed the time saved by skipping review. Pull requests encode the review step into the development process as a mandatory checkpoint rather than an optional courtesy.

Beyond bug prevention, PRs serve as knowledge transfer: reviewers learn about changes before they hit production, preventing knowledge silos. New team members learn codebase conventions by seeing what comments experienced developers leave. PRs also document the rationale for decisions — a comment thread explaining why a particular approach was chosen is invaluable when the code is revisited months later.

For AI systems, PRs are especially important for traceability. Knowing exactly which prompt change caused a degradation in model accuracy, or which retrieval parameter change improved citation quality, requires a detailed change history. PRs provide this at no additional cost when adopted consistently.
Benefits
Pre-merge bug detection:
Human reviewers and automated CI together catch a substantial fraction of bugs before code reaches production.
Knowledge sharing:
Every team member with review access learns about changes before and after they land, reducing knowledge concentration.
Asynchronous collaboration:
Review can happen across time zones without requiring synchronous meetings — comments, approvals, and requests for change all happen asynchronously.
Audit trail:
Every change, every review comment, every approval, and every CI run is permanently recorded against the PR.
CI gate:
Branch protection rules enforce that tests, linters, and security scans pass before any PR can be merged.
Discussion context:
Inline comments on specific lines provide context that commit messages and issue trackers cannot — the review conversation is tied directly to the code it discusses.
Trade-offs
Review latency:
PRs introduce waiting time. In fast-moving teams, slow reviews block progress. Pull request SLA policies (e.g., 24-hour first review response) mitigate this.
Bikeshedding risk:
Reviews can focus on low-value stylistic concerns rather than correctness and architecture. Automated linters should handle style so reviewers can focus on substance.
Context switching:
Reviewing others' PRs interrupts deep work. Batching review time (e.g., morning and afternoon review blocks) reduces this.
Large PR problem:
Very large PRs (>500 lines changed) are effectively unreviable — reviewers approve without reading carefully. Small, focused PRs dramatically improve review quality.
How It Works
The pull request lifecycle

A developer completes work on a feature branch and pushes it to the remote repository. They create a pull request via the platform's web UI, API, or CLI, specifying the source branch, target branch, title, description, and reviewers. The platform automatically detects the diff between the branches and displays it.

CI/CD pipelines are triggered by the PR event. Status checks (test suite, linter, security scanner, type checker) run and their results are reported back to the PR as pass/fail statuses. Branch protection rules can require specific status checks to pass before merge is permitted.

Reviewers examine the diff and leave inline comments on specific lines or general comments on the PR as a whole. They can approve the PR, request changes, or leave a neutral comment. Some platforms allow 'suggestions' — inline diffs that the author can accept and commit with one click.

Once the required number of approvals is reached and all CI checks pass, the PR can be merged. The platform offers merge strategy choices (merge commit, squash, rebase fast-forward) and optionally deletes the source branch automatically.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Pull request lifecycle: author creates PR, CI runs checks, reviewer assigns and comments, author addresses feedback, PR merged and deploy triggered
Anatomy of a high-quality pull request: title format, description sections, and reviewer checklist

content.text: When to Use It
Always use PRs for
Any change to a shared codebase, regardless of size — even single-line fixes benefit from review.
Changes to production configuration, infrastructure-as-code, security policies, or CI/CD pipelines.
Changes to LLM system prompts, model parameters, retrieval configurations, or evaluation thresholds.
Onboarding contributions — new contributors should always go through PR review to learn team conventions.
PR size guidance
Aim for PRs under 400 lines changed. Reviewers can effectively review small, focused changes.
Large features should be decomposed: submit foundational infrastructure as one PR, then feature layers as subsequent PRs.
Draft PRs are appropriate for early feedback on approach — they signal 'not ready to merge, but please comment on direction'.
How to Implement
Creating a PR with the GitHub CLI

# Push your branch
git push -u origin feature/sse-streaming

# Create PR with title and body
gh pr create   --title "feat(streaming): add SSE token streaming for chat completions"   --body "$(cat <<EOF
## Why
Users see a blank screen until the full LLM response arrives. SSE allows
token-by-token streaming, improving perceived performance. Closes #98.

## What changed
- Added /stream FastAPI endpoint using StreamingResponse
- Added EventSource client in frontend/chat.js
- Moved HMAC verification to middleware layer

## Test plan
- [x] Unit tests: 12 new tests in test_streaming.py
- [x] Manual smoke test: Chrome 120, Firefox 121, Safari 17
- [ ] Load test to be completed in staging before final approval

## Breaking changes
None — additive endpoint only.
EOF
)"   --assignee @me   --reviewer team-backend   --label "feature,needs-review"
Creating a PR via GitHub API

import requests, os

GITHUB_TOKEN = os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"]
REPO = "myorg/myrepo"

def create_pull_request(
    title: str,
    body: str,
    head: str,
    base: str = "main",
    draft: bool = False,
) -> dict:
    resp = requests.post(
        f"https://api.github.com/repos/{REPO}/pulls",
        headers={
            "Authorization": f"Bearer {GITHUB_TOKEN}",
            "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json",
        },
        json={
            "title": title,
            "body": body,
            "head": head,
            "base": base,
            "draft": draft,
        },
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()

pr = create_pull_request(
    title="feat(eval): add RAGAS faithfulness metric to eval pipeline",
    body="Adds RAGAS faithfulness scoring to nightly evaluation runs. See #201.",
    head="feature/ragas-eval",
    draft=True,  # open as draft for early feedback
)
print(f"PR #{pr['number']}: {pr['html_url']}")
Adding a CODEOWNERS file
CODEOWNERS automatically assigns reviewers based on file patterns, ensuring the right people are notified for every change:

# .github/CODEOWNERS
# Default owners for everything
*                          @engineering-leads

# Backend services
src/api/**                 @backend-team
src/streaming/**           @backend-team @platform-team

# ML / AI components
src/llm/**                 @ml-team
prompts/**                 @ml-team @product-team
evals/**                   @ml-team

# Infrastructure
infra/**                   @sre-team
.github/workflows/**       @sre-team

# Documentation
docs/**                    @tech-writers
Examples
Example 1 — Automated PR creation for generated content
CI pipelines and AI agents sometimes need to create PRs programmatically — for example, an agent that updates dependency versions or generates API documentation:

import subprocess, os, tempfile
from github import Github  # PyGithub

def create_dependency_update_pr(package: str, new_version: str) -> str:
    """Update a dependency and open a PR automatically."""
    g = Github(os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"])
    repo = g.get_repo("myorg/myrepo")

    branch_name = f"chore/bump-{package.replace('/', '-')}-{new_version}"

    # Get the main branch SHA to create from
    main = repo.get_branch("main")
    repo.create_git_ref(
        ref=f"refs/heads/{branch_name}",
        sha=main.commit.sha,
    )

    # Update requirements.txt (simplified: in practice, use pip-compile or poetry)
    contents = repo.get_contents("requirements.txt", ref=branch_name)
    updated = contents.decoded_content.decode().replace(
        f"{package}==", f"{package}=={new_version}"
    )
    repo.update_file(
        path="requirements.txt",
        message=f"chore: bump {package} to {new_version}",
        content=updated,
        sha=contents.sha,
        branch=branch_name,
    )

    pr = repo.create_pull(
        title=f"chore: bump {package} to {new_version}",
        body=f"Automated dependency update. See changelog: https://pypi.org/project/{package}/{new_version}/",
        head=branch_name,
        base="main",
    )
    return pr.html_url
Example 2 — PR review automation with AI
An AI-assisted code review bot reads the PR diff and posts a structured review comment:

import requests, json, os
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()
GITHUB_TOKEN = os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"]

def get_pr_diff(repo: str, pr_number: int) -> str:
    resp = requests.get(
        f"https://api.github.com/repos/{repo}/pulls/{pr_number}",
        headers={
            "Authorization": f"Bearer {GITHUB_TOKEN}",
            "Accept": "application/vnd.github.v3.diff",
        },
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.text[:8000]  # Limit to first 8K chars

def ai_review_pr(repo: str, pr_number: int) -> str:
    diff = get_pr_diff(repo, pr_number)
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "system",
                "content": (
                    "You are a senior software engineer conducting a code review. "
                    "Review the diff for: (1) correctness bugs, (2) security issues, "
                    "(3) missing error handling. Be concise and specific. "
                    "Format: ### Issue\n**File:Line** Description\n**Suggestion** Fix."
                ),
            },
            {"role": "user", "content": f"Review this diff:\n\n{diff}"},
        ],
        temperature=0,
        max_tokens=800,
    )
    review_body = response.choices[0].message.content

    # Post review comment
    requests.post(
        f"https://api.github.com/repos/{repo}/issues/{pr_number}/comments",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {GITHUB_TOKEN}"},
        json={"body": f"🤖 **AI Review**\n\n{review_body}\n\n*This review is AI-generated and requires human verification.*"},
    )
    return review_body
Example 3 — Merge PR programmatically after checks pass

import requests, os, time

GITHUB_TOKEN = os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"]

def wait_and_merge(repo: str, pr_number: int, merge_method: str = "squash") -> bool:
    """Wait for required checks to pass, then merge the PR."""
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {GITHUB_TOKEN}"}
    pr_url = f"https://api.github.com/repos/{repo}/pulls/{pr_number}"

    for attempt in range(30):  # max 15 minutes
        # Check PR state
        pr = requests.get(pr_url, headers=headers).json()
        if pr["state"] == "closed":
            print("PR already closed")
            return False

        # Check required status checks
        sha = pr["head"]["sha"]
        checks = requests.get(
            f"https://api.github.com/repos/{repo}/commits/{sha}/check-runs",
            headers=headers,
        ).json()

        runs = checks.get("check_runs", [])
        all_passed = all(r["conclusion"] == "success" for r in runs if r["status"] == "completed")
        any_failed = any(r["conclusion"] in ("failure", "cancelled") for r in runs)

        if any_failed:
            print("A check failed — not merging")
            return False

        if all_passed and pr.get("mergeable") and pr.get("mergeable_state") == "clean":
            resp = requests.put(
                f"{pr_url}/merge",
                headers=headers,
                json={"merge_method": merge_method, "commit_title": pr["title"]},
            )
            if resp.status_code == 200:
                print(f"Merged PR #{pr_number}")
                return True

        time.sleep(30)
    print("Timed out waiting for checks")
    return False
Real-World Example

A mid-size AI startup uses GitHub Actions and a PR template to enforce quality gates on every change. The repository has a pull_request_template.md that authors fill in, CODEOWNERS for automatic review assignment, and required status checks including: Python test suite (pytest), type checking (mypy), linting (ruff), dependency security scan (Safety), and a custom LLM eval run on relevant changes.

# .github/pull_request_template.md
## Description
<!-- What does this PR do? Why is this change needed? -->

## Related Issues
<!-- Closes #NNN -->

## Type of change
- [ ] Bug fix (non-breaking)
- [ ] New feature (non-breaking)
- [ ] Breaking change
- [ ] LLM/prompt change
- [ ] Infrastructure/CI change

## Testing
- [ ] Existing tests pass
- [ ] New tests added (if applicable)
- [ ] Manual verification steps: <!-- describe -->

## LLM/AI impact (if applicable)
- [ ] Prompt changes reviewed by ML team
- [ ] Eval scores attached (before/after)
- [ ] Regression tested on golden dataset

## Checklist
- [ ] Self-reviewed my own code
- [ ] No secrets in code or commit history
- [ ] Documentation updated if public API changed
- [ ] Breaking change documented in CHANGELOG.md

# .github/workflows/pr-checks.yml
name: PR Checks
on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: "3.12"}
      - run: pip install -e ".[dev]"
      - run: pytest --cov=src --cov-report=xml -q
      - run: mypy src/
      - run: ruff check src/ tests/

  security:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install safety
      - run: safety check -r requirements.txt

  llm-eval:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'llm-change')
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install -e ".[eval]"
      - run: python scripts/run_evals.py --compare-to-main --output eval-results.json
      - run: python scripts/check_eval_regression.py eval-results.json
Common Mistakes
Mistake 1 — Opening a giant PR with hundreds of unrelated changes

A PR touching 50 files across 4 features is unreviable. Reviewers skim-approve without reading. Bugs slip through. The review discussion becomes unstructured. Break large changes into a stack of small, focused PRs — each one reviewable independently, each building on the previous.
Mistake 2 — Leaving PRs open without updates

A PR opened 3 weeks ago and not updated has likely diverged significantly from main, accumulated many review comments, and its author has context-switched away. Stale PRs accumulate merge debt. Set a policy: PRs with no activity for 5+ days send a reminder notification; PRs with no activity for 2 weeks are closed with a comment to reopen when ready.
Mistake 3 — Approving without reading

Rubber-stamp approvals defeat the purpose of code review. If a reviewer does not have time to review properly, they should decline the review request rather than approve without reading. Require at least two approvals with an expectation that at least one reviewer has examined the change in depth.
Mistake 4 — Posting only negative feedback

Review culture matters. Reviewers who post only corrections send a demoralising signal. Good reviewers also acknowledge clever approaches, note when something is cleaner than they expected, and ask genuine questions rather than framing all questions as implicit criticisms. Use prefixes like nit:, blocker:, question:, and praise: to signal the severity and nature of each comment.
Best Practices
Keep PRs small and focused: aim for under 400 lines changed per PR, covering one logical unit of work.
Write a meaningful PR description: explain WHY (not just WHAT), include a test plan, and reference the relevant issue.
Use a PR template (.github/pull_request_template.md) to prompt authors for all required information.
Set up CODEOWNERS to automatically assign the right reviewers based on the files changed.
Use Draft PRs for early feedback on approach before the implementation is complete.
Address review comments promptly — respond to each comment (fix, explain why not, or request clarification).
Do not merge PRs with unresolved blocking comments or failing required CI checks.
Delete the source branch after merge — automate this with 'auto-delete branch on merge' repository settings.
Enforce branch protection: require at least 1 (ideally 2) approvals and all CI status checks before merge.
Time-box review SLAs: first response within 24 hours, merge-ready within 48 hours for typical PRs.
Performance and Scalability

PR review throughput is a team-level metric as important as test coverage or deployment frequency. High-performing teams (from the DORA metrics research) have lead times (commit to production) of less than one day, which requires PRs to be reviewed and merged within hours, not days.

Reducing PR review cycle time involves: smaller PRs (less to review), clear descriptions (reviewers spend less time understanding context), automated CI (reviewers do not need to run tests manually), and review SLA commitments (no PR waits more than 24 hours for a first review).

Large organisations with many concurrent PRs can use PR queue management tools or AI-assisted routing to balance reviewer load, prevent specific engineers from being overwhelmed, and ensure coverage across time zones. GitHub's auto-assign feature and CODEOWNERS are basic versions of this capability.
Tooling and Ecosystem
GitHub CLI

# List open PRs
gh pr list

# View a specific PR with diff
gh pr view 142 --web

# Check out a PR locally for testing
gh pr checkout 142

# Approve a PR
gh pr review 142 --approve --body "LGTM, great work on the streaming implementation"

# Request changes
gh pr review 142 --request-changes --body "See inline comments — the error handling in the disconnect case needs work"

# Merge when ready
gh pr merge 142 --squash --delete-branch
Pull request labels and automation

# .github/labeler.yml (using actions/labeler)
feature:
  - src/features/**

bug-fix:
  - src/**
  - "!src/features/**"

infrastructure:
  - infra/**
  - .github/**

llm-change:
  - prompts/**
  - src/llm/**
  - evals/**

Security and Reliability
Branch protection rules

Without branch protection, any team member with write access can push directly to main — bypassing review, CI, and all quality gates. Enable branch protection on main and all release branches: require PR, require specified status checks, require approvals, do not allow bypassing restrictions (even for repository admins in high-security environments).
PR-based secret exposure

Secrets committed on a PR branch are visible to anyone with repository read access — not just after merge. Scan for secrets in CI using Gitleaks or GitGuardian as a PR check. If a secret is committed on any branch, rotate it immediately — it should be considered compromised.
Approval bypasses and audit

Branch protection rules should explicitly record who approved each merge and when. In compliance-sensitive environments, use required approvals from specific named teams (not just any team member) and ensure the audit log export is configured. GitHub's audit log API provides a queryable record of all PR events.
Dependency PRs from bots

Dependabot and Renovate automatically open PRs for dependency updates. These PRs should be reviewed as carefully as human-authored PRs — malicious packages have been delivered via typosquatted versions in automated update PRs. Verify dependency changes in a locked, reproducible environment before merging.
Mental Model

A pull request is a formal conversation request attached to a proposed change. The diff is the agenda, the comments are the discussion, the approvals are the votes, and the merge is the decision. Like a formal meeting, it has structure (template), participants (reviewers), a quorum requirement (approval count), and minutes (the permanent PR record).

The Draft PR is the pre-meeting informal discussion: 'Here's my rough idea, tell me if I'm on the right track before I invest more time.' The formal PR is the scheduled meeting: 'I've done the work, please review carefully and vote.' Branch protection rules are the parliamentary procedure: the meeting cannot close (merge) without a quorum and passing vote (required approvals and CI).

Keeping PRs small is like keeping meeting agendas short: the shorter and more focused the agenda, the more substantive the discussion. A meeting with 50 items on the agenda produces rubber-stamp decisions. A meeting with 3 items produces genuine deliberation.
Key Takeaways
A pull request is the platform-level mechanism for proposing, reviewing, and integrating code changes with a permanent audit trail.
Keep PRs small and focused — under 400 lines changed per PR — to enable thorough review rather than rubber-stamp approval.
Write a meaningful description explaining WHY the change is needed, WHAT changed, and how to test it.
Use CODEOWNERS, PR templates, and required status checks to systematise the review process and reduce friction.
Enable branch protection rules: require PR, at least one approval (ideally two), and all CI checks before merge.
Address review comments promptly with a clear response to each: fix, decline with explanation, or ask for clarification.
Draft PRs are appropriate for early feedback on approach before the implementation is complete.
Rotate any secret committed to a PR branch immediately — the secret is exposed to all repository readers as soon as it is pushed.
